medications.drug_name: mechanical haemostasis (firm direct pressure ± silver-nitrate/cautery in person) + protective dressing
medications.indication_rationale: Control the bleeding from the vascular wart base and protect it; keep the dressing dry ~48 hours.

medications.drug_name: topical salicylic acid ± cryotherapy for the remaining warts (routine)
medications.indication_rationale: Definitive routine treatment of the warts (many resolve spontaneously in children).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the dressing dry/intact for ~48 hours, apply firm pressure if it re-bleeds, and stop the child biting nails/chewing the bumps; cover new warts to limit spread.

patient_education_points.point: These are common viral warts. They have a rich blood supply, which is why it bled so much when it tore — but the bleeding is harmless once controlled with pressure (a clinician can seal it with a chemical stick).
Keep the dressing dry for a couple of days. The warts can be treated routinely with wart paint or freezing, and often clear on their own. Stop your child biting/chewing them so they don't spread.
If the dressing soaks through and blood keeps dripping despite 15 minutes of firm pressure, or the finger becomes hot, red and swollen with pus, go to the emergency department.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Healing of the traumatised wart and treatment options for the remaining warts.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Uncontrolled bleeding or infection of the site — urgent/emergency care.

must_include_items: Recognise traumatised common warts (multiple/chronic/verrucous) and distinguish from a pyogenic granuloma
Achieve haemostasis (firm pressure ± in-person cautery) and protect the site
Routine wart treatment (salicylic acid ± cryotherapy) and no-biting/hygiene advice
errors_of_commission: Over-triage to ED for a suspected aggressive/vascular tumour without red flags
Advising the parent to cut/clip the warts at home
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires haemostasis + calibrated reassurance (benign warts vs pyogenic granuloma) + routine wart treatment/hygiene, with an uncontrolled-bleeding/infection safety-net.
